references.title: Predicting Student Performance in a Master of Data Science Program using Admissions Data

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Obtaining a top 20% GPA on a MSc program
extracted.Students: 132 students who enrolled in the MSc
extracted.Data sources: MSc Registration Form: Demographics and Prior Performance
extracted.Features: [GRE Verbal %, GRE Quantitative %, GRE Writing %, TOEFL Total, Months since Degree, Student Age, Marital Status, Gender, Citizenship, School GPA, School Major, School Degree, School Country, School Language, School Rank]
extracted.Moment of Prediction: Time of Admission at the MSc
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Some additional information that would be obtainable from the registration form is not included (e.g. TOEFL subscores or additional schools).

Authors used conditional means to identify potentially relevant features. 

Class imbalance is addressed using Bagging. Authors also use GS to perform hyper-parameter optimization.

Categoricals (e.g. Program are OHE
extracted.Models: Logistic Regression, SVM, CART, Random Forest,  MLP, Naive Bayes, kNN, Voting Ensemble
extracted.Comments:
Authors create separate classifiers while they could have treated this as either regression or Multiclass classification.

Authors also perform a Feature Importance analysis for both models created.

LEarners that come from a more math heavy background are more likely to succeed.
extracted.Limitations:
Double Binary classification which could have been converted into Regression or Multiclass classification. 

Survivorship bias → sample can only consider learners who were accepted in the MSc.

Very small dataset, from one cohort of students. 

Other metrics of evaluation could (and probably should) have been considered.

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Obtaining a bottom 20% GPA on a MSc program
extracted.Students: 132 students who enrolled in the MSc
extracted.Data sources: MSc Registration Form: Demographics and Prior Performance
extracted.Features: [GRE Verbal %, GRE Quantitative %, GRE Writing %, TOEFL Total, Months since Degree, Student Age, Marital Status, Gender, Citizenship, School GPA, School Major, School Degree, School Country, School Language, School Rank]
extracted.Moment of Prediction: Time of Admission at the MSc
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Some additional information that would be obtainable from the registration form is not included (e.g. TOEFL subscores or additional schools).

Authors used conditional means to identify potentially relevant features. 

Class imbalance is addressed using Bagging. Authors also use GS to perform hyper-parameter optimization.

Categoricals (e.g. Program) are OHE
extracted.Models: Logistic Regression, SVM, CART, Random Forest,  MLP, Naive Bayes, kNN, Voting Ensemble
extracted.Comments:
Authors create separate classifiers while they could have treated this as either regression or Multiclass classification.

Authors also perform a Feature Importance analysis for both models created.

Social Sciences candidates are generally poor candidates to the Masters since they are more likely to underperform.
extracted.Limitations:
Double Binary classification which could have been converted into Regression or Multiclass classification. 

Survivorship bias → sample can only consider learners who were accepted in the MSc.

Very small dataset, from one cohort of students. 

Other metrics of evaluation could (and probably should) have been considered.

annotations.text: Authors use Admission Data to predict success of students attending a MSc in Data Science. The paper includes an interpretation of key predictors and multiple classifiers are compared in the task of predicting very good performance from very bad performance on the MSc.
